meta.name: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool. No LLM at runtime. Same answers always produce the same path and reflections.

axes.name: Locus
axes.psychology: Rotter (1954) Locus of Control; Dweck (2006) Growth Mindset

axes.name: Orientation
axes.psychology: Organ (1988) OCB; Campbell et al. (2004) Psychological Entitlement

nodes.text: Good evening. Take a breath. This is five minutes that belong entirely to you — not your inbox, not your tasks. Just an honest look at today.
Before we get into specifics — when you think about today as a whole, which of these comes closest to how it felt?
options: I moved through it — I had a hand in how things went
It moved through me — things happened and I responded
A bit of both, but I mostly held the wheel
A bit of both, but the wheel mostly held me
answer=I moved through it — I had a hand in how things went|A bit of both, but I mostly held the wheel:A1_Q_INT
answer=It moved through me — things happened and I responded|A bit of both, but the wheel mostly held me:A1_Q_EXT

nodes.text: Think of one moment today where something went the way you wanted it to. What was your actual role in that?
options: I had prepared for it — it was no accident
I noticed something shifting and adjusted in the moment
I kept the energy up when others were losing it
Honestly, I'm not sure — it might have just worked out

nodes.text: Think of one moment today where something didn't go as you'd hoped. When you replay it — what's the first thing your mind goes to?
options: Something I could have done differently
What someone else did or didn't do
Circumstances that were just against me today
I'm not sure — it's all a bit tangled
answer=I had prepared for it — it was no accident|I noticed something shifting and adjusted in the moment|I kept the energy up when others were losing it:A1_R_STRONG_INT
answer=Honestly, I'm not sure — it might have just worked out:A1_R_LUCK
answer=Something I could have done differently:A1_R_RECOVERING
answer=What someone else did or didn't do|Circumstances that were just against me today|I'm not sure — it's all a bit tangled:A1_R_EXT

nodes.text: You said "{A0_OPEN.answer}" — and what you just described backs that up. There's a version of today's good moment that just happened to you. But that's not what you described. You were in it. That's not nothing.

You said "{A0_OPEN.answer}" — and yet when the good moment came, your first instinct was to attribute it to luck. That's worth a second look. Luck doesn't explain patterns. What do you keep doing that makes things more likely to go well?
When something went wrong today, your mind went to what *you* could have done differently. That takes honesty. Most people's first move is to find the cause outside themselves. You didn't.

You said "{A0_OPEN.answer}" — and when things went wrong, your attention went outward. That's an honest answer, not a wrong one. But there's usually a small decision buried in there, even in the hard moments. Even choosing to wait is a choice. We'll keep going.
Let's shift from how you moved through the day — to what you gave while you were in it.
At some point today, you interacted with at least one other person — a colleague, a manager, a report, a client. Without naming them: what was the shape of that interaction from your end?
options: I gave something — attention, effort, help, knowledge
I needed something — support, clarity, recognition, time
I showed up to the interaction, but I was somewhere else mentally
I gave, but part of me was keeping score
answer=I gave something — attention, effort, help, knowledge|I gave, but part of me was keeping score:A2_Q_GAVE
answer=I needed something — support, clarity, recognition, time|I showed up to the interaction, but I was somewhere else mentally:A2_Q_NEEDED

nodes.text: When you gave today — what was underneath it? Be honest; no one's grading this.
options: The person needed it and that was enough for me
It was the right thing to do, even though it cost me something
I wanted to be seen as someone who helps
It was automatic — I didn't really think about it

nodes.text: When you think about what you needed today — recognition, support, clarity — did you get it?
options: No, and I'm still carrying that
Partially — enough to keep going, not enough to feel seen
Yes, but it took more effort than it should have
I didn't get it, and I pulled back my effort because of it
answer=The person needed it and that was enough for me|It was the right thing to do, even though it cost me something:A2_R_PURE
answer=I wanted to be seen as someone who helps|It was automatic — I didn't really think about it:A2_R_MIXED
answer=No, and I'm still carrying that|Partially — enough to keep going, not enough to feel seen|Yes, but it took more effort than it should have:A2_R_ENTITLED_HONEST
answer=I didn't get it, and I pulled back my effort because of it:A2_R_ENTITLED_WITHDREW

nodes.text: What you described — giving because someone needed it, or because it was right — is the kind of contribution that builds trust without announcing itself. It's not heroic. It's just solid. Teams that run on that don't usually know how much they rely on it.

You gave today. The motivation was mixed — reputation, habit, something else. That's completely normal. The question isn't whether the motive was pure. The question is: did the giving happen? It did. That's the thing that landed in someone else's day.

You needed something today and didn't fully get it. That's a real gap, not a complaint. The interesting question is what you did with it — you kept going. That costs something. Worth knowing that about yourself.

When recognition didn't arrive, you pulled back. That's an honest thing to admit. It's also a pattern worth watching — because the withdrawal usually costs you more than it costs the person who didn't give you what you needed.
Last shift. From what you gave — to who was in your mind while you were doing it.
When today was hardest — in the moment when you felt the most pressure or frustration — where did your mind go?
options: To me — my deadline, my reputation, my stress
To my team — how this was landing on them
To a specific person who was having a harder time than me
To the bigger picture — what we're all actually trying to do
answer=To me — my deadline, my reputation, my stress:A3_Q_SELF
answer=To my team — how this was landing on them|To a specific person who was having a harder time than me|To the bigger picture — what we're all actually trying to do:A3_Q_OTHER

nodes.text: Being focused on yourself in a hard moment is human. Did anyone around you notice you were struggling today — or were you carrying it alone?
options: I kept it entirely to myself
People probably noticed, but I didn't say anything
I tried to protect the people around me from it
Someone asked, and I told them something close to the truth

nodes.text: You said your mind went to others when things were hard. Did that awareness change anything about how you acted — or did it mostly change how you felt?
options: It changed what I did — I made a different call because of them
It changed how I felt, but I acted the same way I would have anyway
It made the day harder — I was carrying their load on top of mine
It made things easier — their context made my own feel smaller
answer=I tried to protect the people around me from it:A3_R_PROTECTIVE
answer=I kept it entirely to myself|People probably noticed, but I didn't say anything|Someone asked, and I told them something close to the truth:A3_R_SELF
answer=It changed what I did — I made a different call because of them|It made things easier — their context made my own feel smaller:A3_R_TRANSCENDENT
answer=It changed how I felt, but I acted the same way I would have anyway|It made the day harder — I was carrying their load on top of mine:A3_R_AWARE

nodes.text: Absorbing difficulty so others don't have to is a quiet form of care. It often goes unnoticed. The question worth sitting with: are you protecting them because it helps — or because it means you don't have to show the struggle yourself?

You carried today mostly internally. That takes a kind of endurance. But there's a version of this that becomes a habit — and a habit of self-containment can slowly narrow the frame until it's just you and your problem, without the context of everyone else's day around you.

Awareness of others that actually changes your behavior is rarer than it sounds. Most people feel for others and then act exactly as they would have anyway. You didn't. That gap — between noticing and doing — is where character lives.

You kept others in view even when you were under pressure. It didn't change what you did, but it changed how you experienced the day — either as a burden or a frame. Both are real responses to genuinely caring about more than yourself.

axis1.internal: your attention went toward what you could shape. That's agency — not control, not perfection, just orientation.
axis1.external: your attention went outward today — toward what others did, what circumstances prevented, what should have been different. That's worth sitting with overnight.

axis2.contribution: you gave something today without making it a transaction. That's the kind of effort that compounds quietly.
axis2.entitlement: you were tracking the gap between what you gave and what you received. That gap is real. The question is what you do while it exists.

axis3.altrocentric: even under pressure, your frame included other people. That's not nothing — it's the difference between a hard day and a lonely one.
axis3.selfcentric: your frame today was tight — your own load, your own pressure. That's sometimes necessary. Just don't let it become the default.

nodes.text: See you tomorrow.
